title: Mucking out a pig arc
subtitle: Keeping the arc bedding dry and the sleeping area clean for two to four outdoor weaners.
excerpt: A damp, fouled arc cools pigs at night, increases respiratory disease risk, and adds to the worm burden on the ground. Pigs naturally dung away from their sleeping area if given enough space; the keeper's job is to maintain the dry bedding that makes this separation worth doing. This covers the weekly muck-out and the deep-litter alternative for winter.
sourceNotes:
AHDB Pork, Outdoor Pig Production guide. https://ahdb.org.uk
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Pigs (2003). https://www.gov.uk

glossaryTerms.definition: A bedding management method in which straw is added progressively on top of accumulated dung and older bedding rather than fully removed each week. The decomposing lower layers generate some heat, which can be useful in cold weather. Deep litter must be turned or removed before it becomes anaerobic and compacted.

The site where dung and soiled bedding is stacked to compost. Pig muck is classed as agricultural waste and must be managed under farm waste management rules. It must not be applied to land within 50 metres of a watercourse. Well-composted pig muck after six months is a useful soil improver.

content.text: The arc should be mucked out completely once a week in summer and topped up with fresh straw every two to three days. In winter, a
approach reduces heat loss: add 5 to 10 cm of fresh straw on top of the existing bedding every two to three days and remove and replace the whole bed every three to four weeks.

Feed pigs at the far end of the paddock before you start mucking out: pigs are extremely curious and will investigate anything moving near the arc while you work. A pig between your legs and a wheelbarrow in a small arc is uncomfortable for both of you. Once pigs are occupied eating, you have ten to fifteen minutes to work quickly.

Remove all soiled bedding into a barrow. Hose or brush out any dried dung from the arc floor. Leave the arc to air for 30 minutes before re-bedding. Add a generous layer of fresh dry straw: pigs need at least 10 to 15 cm of straw to root and nest in, and a thin layer is quickly compressed to nothing. The soiled bedding goes onto the
and should not be spread on land for at least six months.

When you rotate pigs to a fresh paddock, move the arc with them. The ground under and around a static arc builds up a heavy worm-egg burden rapidly. If the arc cannot be moved (because it is a fixed structure), allow a minimum of 12 months rest on the old site before running pigs on it again. Arc doors should face away from the prevailing wind to prevent driving rain and cold entering while pigs sleep.
